title: The User Authentication — == vs .equals() for Strings
guidelines: The response MUST identify that `==` compares object references (memory addresses), not string content
Should explain that `new String("secret123")` creates a new object on the heap, distinct from the interned string literal `"secret123"`

Should mention Java's String interning: literal strings are pooled and `==` works by accident with two literals, but breaks when strings come from constructors, user input, database queries, or network I/O
The fix must replace `==` with `.equals()` or `Objects.equals()` for value comparison
Bonus: mentions that `Objects.equals()` is null-safe and is the recommended modern approach

responses.label: Response A
Response B
Response C

dimensions.name: Root Cause Identification
dimensions.description: Does it correctly identify reference vs value comparison as the root cause?

dimensions.name: Explanation Depth
dimensions.description: Does it explain String interning, heap allocation, and why == works with literals?

dimensions.name: Fix Correctness
dimensions.description: Is the fix correct, robust, and does it solve the general problem (not just this test case)?

dimensions.name: Null Safety
dimensions.description: Does it address potential NullPointerException when calling .equals() on a possibly null reference?

dimensions.name: Educational Value
dimensions.description: Would this help a developer avoid == for objects in all future code?

title: The Order Processor — ConcurrentModificationException
guidelines: The response MUST explain that the enhanced for-loop uses an Iterator internally, and calling `orders.remove()` modifies the list's structural modification count, causing the iterator to detect the mismatch and throw ConcurrentModificationException
Should explain this is Java's fail-fast mechanism — it detects concurrent structural modification even in single-threaded code
The fix should use one of: Iterator.remove(), removeIf(), a stream filter, or iterate over a copy
Penalize responses that only say 'don't modify during iteration' without explaining the internal mechanism
Bonus: mentions that removeIf() (Java 8+) is the most concise and idiomatic modern solution

responses.label: Response A
Response B
Response C
responses.text:
The for-each loop creates an iterator, and calling `remove()` on the list directly invalidates it. The safest modern fix is `removeIf()`:

```java
orders.removeIf(order -> order.startsWith("CANCELLED"));
```

Alternatively, you can use `Iterator.remove()` which keeps the iterator in sync with the list. The key insight is that `List.remove()` and `Iterator.remove()` are different methods — only the latter is safe during iteration.

dimensions.name: Root Cause Identification
dimensions.description: Does it correctly identify the Iterator/modCount fail-fast mechanism?

dimensions.name: Explanation Depth
dimensions.description: Does it explain why the enhanced for-loop uses an Iterator and how modCount works?

dimensions.name: Fix Correctness
dimensions.description: Is the proposed fix correct and free of its own bugs (e.g., index-skip bug)?

dimensions.name: Modern Java
dimensions.description: Does it suggest removeIf() or stream-based approaches as idiomatic Java 8+ solutions?

dimensions.name: Educational Value
dimensions.description: Would this help a developer understand and avoid ConcurrentModificationException?

title: The Tag Manager — Arrays.asList() Fixed-Size Trap
guidelines: The response MUST explain that Arrays.asList() returns a fixed-size List backed by the original array — it supports set() but not add() or remove()
Should clarify that the returned list is NOT a java.util.ArrayList; it is java.util.Arrays$ArrayList, a different class with different behavior
The fix must wrap Arrays.asList() in a new ArrayList constructor, or use List.of() with an explicit new ArrayList(), or use other mutable list construction
Penalize responses that confuse unmodifiable (List.of) with fixed-size (Arrays.asList) — they are different constraints
Bonus: mentions List.of() (Java 9+) which returns a fully immutable list vs Arrays.asList() which allows set() but not structural changes

responses.label: Response A
responses.text:
This is the **`Arrays.asList()` fixed-size list** trap — a classic Java gotcha.

**What's happening:** `Arrays.asList("general", "public", "active")` does NOT return a `java.util.ArrayList`. It returns a `java.util.Arrays$ArrayList` — a **fixed-size** list backed by the original array. This inner class:

- **Allows** `set()` (replacing elements)
- **Forbids** `add()` and `remove()` (structural modification) → throws `UnsupportedOperationException`

This is because the list is just a thin wrapper around the array, and arrays in Java have a fixed length.

**Fix — wrap in a real ArrayList:**

```java
public static List<String> initializeTags(String... extraTags) {
    List<String> tags = new ArrayList<>(Arrays.asList("general", "public", "active"));
    for (String tag : extraTags) {
        tags.add(tag);
    }
    return tags;
}
```

`new ArrayList<>(Arrays.asList(...))` copies the elements into a true `java.util.ArrayList` that supports all operations.

**Java 9+ alternative:**

Note that `List.of("general", "public", "active")` is even more restrictive — it's **fully immutable** (no `set()`, no `add()`, no `remove()`, no `null` elements). To make it mutable:

```java
List<String> tags = new ArrayList<>(List.of("general", "public", "active"));
```

**Summary of list types:**

| Factory | Type | set() | add()/remove() | null elements |
|---------|------|-------|----------------|---------------|
| `Arrays.asList()` | Fixed-size | Yes | No | Yes |
| `List.of()` (Java 9+) | Immutable | No | No | No |
| `new ArrayList<>()` | Mutable | Yes | Yes | Yes |

responses.label: Response B
responses.text:
`Arrays.asList()` returns an immutable list. You need to create a mutable copy:

```java
List<String> tags = new ArrayList<>(Arrays.asList("general", "public", "active"));
```

This creates a regular ArrayList that you can add to and remove from.

responses.label: Response C

dimensions.name: Root Cause Identification
dimensions.description: Does it correctly identify that Arrays.asList() returns a fixed-size (not immutable) list?

dimensions.name: Explanation Depth
dimensions.description: Does it explain the difference between Arrays$ArrayList and java.util.ArrayList?

dimensions.name: Fix Correctness
dimensions.description: Is the fix correct and idiomatic?

dimensions.name: Nuance
dimensions.description: Does it distinguish fixed-size (Arrays.asList) from immutable (List.of) from mutable (ArrayList)?

dimensions.name: Educational Value
dimensions.description: Would this help a developer understand Java's different List implementations?

title: The Invoice Calculator — float/double Precision for Money
guidelines: The response MUST explain that double/float use IEEE 754 binary floating-point, which cannot exactly represent most decimal fractions (like 0.1, 0.07, etc.)
Should explain that these tiny representation errors accumulate across operations, producing visible rounding artifacts in financial outputs
The fix must use BigDecimal with String constructors (not double constructors) for monetary values
Penalize responses that just round the output (formatting) without fixing the underlying computation
Bonus: mentions BigDecimal.valueOf() as a safer alternative to the String constructor, and explains the need for explicit RoundingMode

responses.label: Response A
Response B
Response C

dimensions.description: Does it correctly identify IEEE 754 binary representation as the root cause?

dimensions.name: Explanation Depth
dimensions.description: Does it explain WHY double can't represent 0.1 exactly and how errors accumulate?

dimensions.name: Fix Correctness
dimensions.description: Does the fix use BigDecimal correctly (String constructors, RoundingMode)?

dimensions.name: BigDecimal Pitfalls
dimensions.description: Does it warn about BigDecimal's own pitfalls (double constructor, equals vs compareTo)?

dimensions.name: Educational Value
dimensions.description: Would this help a developer understand why float/double is unsuitable for money?

title: The Employee Registry — equals() Without hashCode()
guidelines: The response MUST identify the missing hashCode() override as the root cause
Should explain the equals/hashCode contract: if two objects are equal according to equals(), they MUST have the same hashCode()
Should explain how HashSet works: it uses hashCode() to find the bucket FIRST, then equals() to check for duplicates within that bucket
Without matching hashCode(), the two Employee objects land in different buckets and equals() is never called
The fix must override hashCode() using the same fields used in equals() (id in this case)

responses.label: Response A
Response B
Response C

dimensions.name: Explanation Depth
dimensions.description: Does it explain the bucket-then-equals mechanism of hash-based collections?

dimensions.name: Fix Correctness
dimensions.description: Is the hashCode() implementation correct and consistent with equals()?

dimensions.name: Contract Knowledge
dimensions.description: Does it clearly state the equals/hashCode contract and its consequences?

dimensions.name: Educational Value
dimensions.description: Would this help a developer always override both methods together?

title: The Survey Analyzer — Autoboxing Null NullPointerException
guidelines: The response MUST identify that the comparison `rating >= 1` triggers unboxing of the Integer to int, and unboxing a null Integer throws NullPointerException
Should explain Java's autoboxing/unboxing mechanism: Integer (object) ↔ int (primitive), and that null cannot be unboxed
Should explain why the NPE appears on a line with no visible method call — the unboxing is implicit
The fix must add a null check before any operation that would trigger unboxing
Bonus: mentions Integer caching (-128 to 127) and that == between Integer objects can also surprise developers

responses.label: Response A
Response B
Response C

dimensions.description: Does it correctly identify implicit unboxing of null Integer as the root cause?

dimensions.name: Explanation Depth
dimensions.description: Does it explain autoboxing/unboxing, why NPE appears on a comparison line, and the invisible intValue() call?

dimensions.name: Fix Correctness
dimensions.description: Is the fix correct and does it prevent unboxing of null values?

dimensions.name: Short-Circuit Awareness
dimensions.description: Does it explain how short-circuit && ensures the null check protects against unboxing?

dimensions.description: Would this help a developer recognize autoboxing NPE risks with Integer, Long, Boolean, etc.?

title: The File Processor — Swallowed Exception in Empty Catch Block
guidelines: The response MUST identify the empty catch block as the primary bug — it swallows the exception and hides the failure
Should also identify the missing try-with-resources — the BufferedReader is not properly closed if an exception occurs during reading
Should explain that catching broad Exception is an anti-pattern; catching specific exceptions (FileNotFoundException, IOException) is preferred
The fix should use try-with-resources for the reader AND either log/rethrow or handle the exception meaningfully
Bonus: discusses fail-fast philosophy — silent failures cause cascading bugs that are much harder to diagnose than immediate crashes

responses.label: Response A
Response B
Response C

dimensions.description: Does it identify both the empty catch block and the resource leak?

dimensions.name: Explanation Depth
dimensions.description: Does it explain why silent failures are dangerous and the fail-fast principle?

dimensions.name: Fix Correctness
dimensions.description: Does the fix use try-with-resources and handle exceptions meaningfully?

dimensions.description: Does it recommend catching specific exceptions instead of broad Exception?

dimensions.name: Educational Value
dimensions.description: Would this help a developer write robust error handling and never swallow exceptions?

title: The Report Builder — Stream Reuse IllegalStateException
guidelines: The response MUST explain that Java streams can only be consumed ONCE — after a terminal operation (like count()), the stream is closed and cannot be reused
Should explain the stream lifecycle: source → intermediate operations (lazy) → terminal operation (consumes) → stream closed
The fix should either create two separate streams from the source, compute both results in a single pass, or collect to a list first and create streams from that
Penalize responses that suggest 'resetting' the stream or don't explain why streams are single-use
Bonus: compares Java streams (single-use) to Python generators (also single-use) for cross-language understanding

responses.label: Response A
Response B
responses.text:
Streams can only be used once. Just create a new stream for each operation:

```java
long salesCount = transactions.stream()
    .filter(t -> t.startsWith("SALE")).count();

double total = transactions.stream()
    .filter(t -> t.startsWith("SALE"))
    .map(t -> t.split(":")[1].trim())
    .mapToDouble(Double::parseDouble).sum();
```

Each call to `.stream()` creates a fresh stream from the list.

responses.label: Response C

dimensions.name: Explanation Depth

dimensions.name: Fix Correctness
dimensions.description: Is the proposed fix correct and does it avoid the reuse problem?

dimensions.name: Efficiency Awareness
dimensions.description: Does it discuss trade-offs between separate streams and single-pass approaches?

dimensions.name: Educational Value
dimensions.description: Would this help a developer understand stream lifecycle and avoid reuse errors?

title: The Database Connection — Resource Leak Without try-with-resources
guidelines: The response MUST explain that if an exception occurs during executeQuery() or while processing the ResultSet, the close() calls at the bottom are never reached, leaking the Connection, PreparedStatement, and ResultSet
Should explain that try-with-resources (Java 7+) guarantees closure in the finally block, even when exceptions occur
The fix must convert to try-with-resources with nested resource declarations
Should note that resources in try-with-resources are closed in reverse declaration order, and suppressed exceptions are handled properly
Bonus: mentions connection pooling (HikariCP, etc.) as the production-grade solution for managing database connections

responses.label: Response A
Response B
Response C

dimensions.description: Does it correctly identify that close() is skipped when exceptions occur?

dimensions.name: Explanation Depth
dimensions.description: Does it explain the exception-path resource leak and why manual close() is fragile?

dimensions.name: Fix Correctness
dimensions.description: Does the fix use try-with-resources correctly with proper nesting?

dimensions.name: Resource Management Knowledge
dimensions.description: Does it explain suppressed exceptions and reverse-order closing?

dimensions.name: Educational Value
